concise:
Ayah 1–14 · linked video: 01. At-Takwir (Ayah 1-14)

Surah At-Takwir: Commentary on Ayahs 1-14

Ayah 1

إِذَا ٱلشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ

The surah opens with a striking grammatical construction that immediately captures attention. The word إِذَا means “when,” and in Arabic, it specifically refers to something that will happen in the future—not a mere possibility, but a certainty. This is Allah’s ﷻ way of declaring that these events are bound to occur.

The expected Arabic construction would have been “kuwwirat ash-shams” (the sun was folded), but instead we find “ash-shamsu kuwwirat” (the sun, it was folded). This unusual word order communicates shock and amazement. It’s as if the ayah is saying: “Even the sun—yes, the sun itself—will be folded up!” This carries the weight of two exclamation marks in English.

The verb كُوِّرَتْ comes from the practice of folding and wrapping turbans. When something is folded up, you cannot see what’s inside—it becomes hidden and obscured. On the Day of Judgment, when people look up at the sun, it will appear to be getting wrapped up and disappearing. The light that fills our days, that we take for granted, will be folding in on itself. This also refers to the sunlight itself dwindling and the planet plunging into sudden darkness.

The use of the past tense verb for a future event serves a powerful rhetorical purpose. It’s like saying in English, “Consider it done”—using past tense to guarantee something in the future. This is Allah’s ﷻ way of emphasizing that this event is not just possible, but inevitable and imminent.

Ayah 2

وَإِذَا ٱلنُّجُومُ ٱنكَدَرَتْ

As the sun folds up and darkness descends, people naturally look to the stars for light. But this ayah tells us that even the stars will fail us on that Day.

The word انكَدَرَتْ carries multiple layers of meaning. The root word relates to something becoming dirty, polluted, or discolored. Water that has gone bad and changed color is described with this same root. The stars that appear as pure white lights to us will begin to look dirty and discolored.

But there’s a second, more dramatic meaning: انكَدَرَتْ also means to be plucked out suddenly. It’s used to describe when a horse’s hoof strikes the beach sand and sends grains flying into the air—a sudden, forceful removal. The stars will appear to be plucked right out of the sky, disappearing before our eyes.

The word also conveys speed and intensity. When we see distant objects in space, they appear to move slowly even when traveling at incredible speeds. But the imagery here suggests that even from our galactic vantage point, we’ll see them falling rapidly, like raindrops—an impossible sight that testifies to the magnitude of that Day.

Ayah 3

وَإِذَا ٱلْجِبَالُ سُيِّرَتْ

Now the focus shifts from the sky to the earth. While the cosmic events above preoccupy us, something equally dramatic unfolds beneath our feet: even the mountains will be made to move away.

Mountains are mentioned repeatedly throughout the Quran in descriptions of the Day of Judgment—more than any other earthly feature. They will be crushed into sand, flattened, pulverized, and made to sail away. This repetition is significant because mountains are the most prominent and stable structures on our planet. They are symbols of permanence and strength.

Mountains play a crucial role in our planet’s ecology. Glaciers melt from mountain peaks to feed rivers and lakes. Mountain ranges affect weather patterns, directing clouds and winds. They separate bodies of water from land, keeping entire regions from being submerged. If the mountains are flattened, what happens to the oceans? What happens to the land that depends on them for protection?

The word سُيِّرَتْ suggests smooth movement, like something sailing away. These massive, immovable structures will glide away as if weightless, creating space for what comes next in this sequence of catastrophic events.

Ayah 4

وَإِذَا ٱلْعِشَارُ عُطِّلَتْ

Now Allah ﷻ brings the scene back to something the Arabs could personally relate to: their most valuable possession.

The word العِشَار refers to a pregnant she-camel in her tenth month of pregnancy, ready to give birth at any moment. In desert Arab society, camels were not just animals—they were vehicles, cargo carriers, wealth, and survival itself. A camel was like owning a truck that could traverse impossible terrain and sustain itself for long journeys.

When a she-camel was pregnant, Arabs would go out of their way to care for it. They wouldn’t load anything on it, would keep it in shade, give it the best food and water—because that animal represented a doubling of their investment. They would rather sleep outside themselves than let this precious camel be uncomfortable. This was their most valuable asset, the thing they protected above all else.

The word عُطِّلَتْ means to be left abandoned and uncared for. When something valuable is uttilat, its appearance becomes scruffy and neglected—like a person going through severe depression who stops caring for themselves, wearing no makeup, no jewelry, looking disheveled.

Picture this: a pregnant camel, the most pampered and protected creature in Arab society, is wandering around alone, looking neglected, clearly not cared for. This would be as shocking to them as seeing someone leave a luxury car running with the doors open in a rough neighborhood for days. Who would do such a thing? Only someone who has much bigger problems.

The word ’ishar had cultural significance beyond just pregnant camels. Arabs used this same word for dark rain clouds, because rain in the desert was as rare and precious as a pregnant camel. Both were exotic treasures. So ’ishar represented anything of tremendous value in their culture.

For us today, al-’isharu uttilat could mean: mansions left with gates open, stoves running, cars with keys in the ignition and doors open, but nobody there. Bank vaults opened with gold coins spilling out, and nobody caring. Safety deposit boxes left open and unattended. This is utterly impossible to imagine—it only happens when something far more terrifying has everyone’s attention.

Ayah 5

وَإِذَا ٱلْوُحُوشُ حُشِرَتْ

The word الوُحُوش refers to wild animals—predators that show no mercy. Tigers, lions, pythons, alligators—creatures you don’t want to be near. On that Day, even these fierce animals will be herded together.

The scene described is extraordinary: the lion standing next to the sheep, standing next to human beings, and none of them attacking each other. They’re too terrified to care about their natural instincts. This has actually been witnessed in earthly disasters—during great floods and earthquakes, natural predators and prey have been seen running side by side or perched together on high ground, their survival instinct overriding everything else.

When the oceans are boiling and the earth is being torn apart, wild animals and people are being herded in the same direction, running from the same catastrophe. Their usual hierarchy of predator and prey means nothing anymore. They’re making room for each other because appetite has vanished in the face of absolute terror.

Ayah 6

وَإِذَا ٱلْبِحَارُ سُجِّرَتْ

Now we understand where everyone—humans and animals—is running. They’re fleeing from the oceans.

The word سُجِّرَتْ means the oceans will be filled up and merged together. When the mountains have been flattened and the earth has been stretched, the different bodies of water that were separated by land masses will flow into one another. The land depressions left by the absent mountains will fill with water, creating massive tsunami waves herding everything before them.

But there’s more to this word. When the earth gets stretched, it will tear open—particularly at the ocean floors. Underneath the earth’s crust lies molten lava, the core of the planet. When the ocean floor tears open, this lava will pour into the oceans, turning them into giant boiling pots.

Another ayah describes this as سُعِّرَتْ —when the oceans boil over and explode. Think of a kettle with the lid on, heat on high, no room for steam to escape. It will explode. That’s the kind of violent, scorching explosion awaiting the oceans. First they merge, then they boil, then they explode. People and animals are running from walls of water that are also walls of fire.

Ayah 7

وَإِذَا ٱلنُّفُوسُ زُوِّجَتْ

In the midst of this chaos—mountains moving, oceans boiling, animals and humans fleeing together—souls will be paired up. But what does this mean?

The word زُوِّجَتْ comes from zawj, meaning a pair or spouse. This ayah has multiple layers of meaning, each revealing something profound about the Day of Judgment.

First, when we die, our body and soul are separated. The soul departs and ascends for judgment, while the body remains in the grave. On the Day of Resurrection, these two will be reunited. You won’t return as some ethereal spiritual being—you will be fully physical again, body and soul merged as you were in life. The Quran has no concept of disembodied spiritual existence after resurrection. The story of the People of the Cave actually settled a Christian theological debate of that time, proving that Allah ﷻ will resurrect both body and soul together.

Second, each person will be paired with their qarin—their personal devil who has been studying them their entire life, knowing exactly which temptations work on them and when. This companion has been whispering, suggesting, making evil seem attractive. On that Day, you’ll be brought together with this devil who will testify against you. The Quran addresses this in Surah Qaf, where the person argues with their qarin, shocked at this betrayal. But the devil was never your friend—he only pretended to be. Those feelings you thought were yours? They were planted. That’s why Allah ﷻ keeps repeating in the Quran: “Indeed, the devil is your enemy, so take him as your enemy.”

Third, people will be grouped with others like them—sorted by their qualities and actions in this life. The greedy with the greedy, the generous with the generous, the sincere with the sincere. You’ll be paired with those who shared your values and priorities. The Quran mentions three major groups on Judgment Day: those who raced ahead in goodness, the people of the right hand, and the people of the left hand.

This concept of azwaj (groups) applies to this life too. There are social circles of wealthy people who compete over who has the better house, the more expensive car, who can host the more impressive dinner party. These become their azwaj—their group, their identity. The Prophet ﷺ was warned not to look longingly at certain azwaj who had abundance in this life. The people you choose to be with in this world often indicate who you’ll be grouped with in the next.

The quality that defines you most prominently might be what you’re grouped by. If you were known for anger, you’ll be with the angry. If you were known for charity, you’ll be with the charitable. Your life’s defining characteristic becomes your eternal association.

Ayah 8-9

وَإِذَا ٱلْمَوْءُۥدَةُ سُئِلَتْ بِأَىِّ ذَنۢبٍ قُتِلَتْ

In the midst of all this cosmic chaos—the collapsing sky, the boiling oceans, the herding of terrified creatures—Allah ﷻ suddenly focuses on one small, innocent victim: the baby girl who was buried alive.

The word المَوْءُودَة specifically refers to female infants who were killed in pre-Islamic Arabia. This was a practice born of twisted pride and economic calculation. Some Arab men considered having a daughter a challenge to their manhood—as if fathering a son proved virility while having a daughter proved weakness. Others feared that daughters were liabilities: “If she marries into another tribe, they’ll have leverage over me. They can use her against me in business dealings.”

These men had two cruel options. The less barbaric was to force the mother and daughter into a separate tent where they would live in permanent isolation, never to be seen by the father again. He would leave food outside, but would never look at them, treating his wife and daughter as cursed. There’s heartbreaking poetry from one of these abandoned mothers, singing to her baby: “What’s wrong with Abu Hamza that he never visits? He’s angry we didn’t give him a boy. But by God, that wasn’t in our hands. We women are like the earth—we can only give back what you planted in us.”

The more horrific practice was to take the infant girl out to the desert, force the mother to separate from her child, dig a pit, and bury the girl alive while she screamed. This was his way of “proving” his manhood—that he could withstand her cries and screams as he shoveled dirt onto his own daughter.

This is what al-maw’udah means—and it comes from a root word suggesting loud, screeching noises, the kind that ring in your head and haunt you. On Judgment Day, that screaming will be remembered.

The ayah asks: بِأَىِّ ذَنۢبٍ قُتِلَتْ—“For what sin was she killed?” The word ذَنۢب comes from the word for “tail,” and in Arabic poetry, your dignity resides in your nose (the highest point), while the tail represents humiliation (the lowest point). So dhanb refers specifically to sins that humiliate and embarrass. What humiliating crime did this innocent baby commit that she deserved to be killed?

The question is devastating in its simplicity. This child will be asked—not her murderer, but her. She will be given a voice when she was given no voice in life. And the question implies its own answer: there is no sin, no crime, no justification whatsoever.

Consider another ayah about this practice: “When one of them receives news of a baby girl, his face becomes darkened and he is filled with inward grief. He hides himself from the people because of the ill he has been given news of. Should he keep her in humiliation, or bury her in the ground?” These men saw their daughters as humiliation, something shameful to hide.

But this crime wasn’t just committed by pagans. Today, there are many ways to bury girls alive. When boys in a family receive education but girls are denied it—that’s burying her alive. When a girl is forced into marriage against her will, told “you must marry your cousin or you’re not my daughter anymore, you’ll have the curse of Allah ﷻ for disobeying your parents”—that’s burying her alive. That nikah, by the way, is null and void in the sight of Allah ﷻ. No one can be forced into marriage, and no one can be trapped in a marriage they don’t want. Threatening them with disownment or family honor is the same sick mentality that led to female infanticide.

When parents see daughters as burdens to marry off quickly, when mothers pressure their daughters-in-law to produce grandsons, when women themselves perpetuate this discrimination against other women—these are modern forms of burying girls alive.

The Prophet ﷺ said that whoever has three daughters or three sisters and treats them well will enter Paradise. Your daughters are your ticket to Heaven, yet some Muslims still see them as less valuable than sons. How can anyone who follows Muhammad ﷺ think this way?

Among all the cataclysmic events of Judgment Day, Allah ﷻ gives voice to the voiceless: the baby girl who was never allowed to speak. This tells us that Allah ﷻ cares about all oppression, not just oppression against Muslims. The Quran came to defend humanity, to stand against all injustice. When we see child abuse, addiction, violence, depression, suicide in society and say “that’s the problem of the kuffar,” we haven’t understood the Quran’s message at all.

Ayah 10

وَإِذَا ٱلصُّحُفُ نُشِرَتْ

After the mountains have moved, creating space, and after the innocent have been given their voice, the scrolls will be laid open.

The word نُشِرَتْ means to spread out, to unfold, to display openly. These are the records of every human being’s life—what the Quran calls kitab in other places, the book of deeds. Each person’s scroll contains everything they did, said, thought, and intended.

The timing is significant. The mountains have made way—physically creating room for these countless scrolls to be spread out. The environmental chaos has settled enough for the judgment to begin. These scrolls have always existed; angels have been recording every detail. But now they will be opened and read.

What’s profound is that later ayahs tell us these scrolls will be handed to people—some in their right hand, others in their left hand or behind their back. If it’s in your left hand, you know you’re in trouble. If it’s behind your back, you don’t even know how much trouble you’re in because you can’t see it. But as we’ll learn at the end of these ayahs, you don’t actually need to read the scroll to know what’s in it, because on that Day, your memory will be complete.

Ayah 11

وَإِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ كُشِطَتْ

We began with the sun folding up and the stars falling. Now the entire sky will be torn away.

The word كُشِطَتْ means to peel skin off an animal, to strip away a covering. It’s a violent image—like peeling hide from a carcass. The sky that we see as solid and permanent will be ripped away like a thin membrane.

Another ayah describes the sky turning red like melted metal, like the color of blood-stained animal skin. The image Allah ﷻ uses repeatedly is of the sky as a helpless domesticated animal being slaughtered. To Allah ﷻ, the entire heavens are as vulnerable as a single creature being skinned.

The progression is deliberate: first the sun folds up, then the stars fall, then the whole sky is peeled away. Layer by layer, everything we thought was permanent and unchanging is stripped away. The cosmic order we depend on completely comes undone.

Ayah 12

وَإِذَا ٱلْجَحِيمُ سُعِّرَتْ

When the Hellfire is set ablaze—but this requires deeper explanation because Hell already exists.

The word الجَحِيم is one of the names of Hell in the Quran. It comes from the root word related to price, value, and evaluation. When you test how combustible something is, how well it burns, that’s related to this root.

The word سُعِّرَتْ suggests putting something to the test, pushing it to its limits. Hell has always existed—it was created long ago, just as Paradise already exists. But on that Day, Allah ﷻ will excite Hell with a new, fresh flame. It’s like adding fuel to an already burning fire and watching it roar to life with renewed intensity.

Think of it this way: Hell has been “waiting” in a sense. On Judgment Day, it will be unleashed, set ablaze with a fervor it hasn’t displayed before. The fire that was will become the Fire that is—fully activated, fully realized, terrifyingly eager.

Ayah 13

وَإِذَا ٱلْجَنَّةُ أُزْلِفَتْ

In stark contrast to Hell being set ablaze, Paradise will be brought near.

The word أُزْلِفَتْ means to be brought close, elevated, made visible. Picture this: the sun doesn’t exist anymore, stars have fallen, oceans are boiling, the earth is torn open with lava emerging, and Hell itself has been set ablaze below. In the midst of all this chaos and darkness, there appears a lush, calm garden in full view.

There’s likely a spatial element to this imagery. Hell is described as being below—we know from other ayahs that people will walk over it. Paradise is described as having elevated ranks, ascending levels. People in Paradise will be able to look down at Hell. So when Allah ﷻ says Paradise will be brought close, it suggests it will be elevated and brought into view.

Imagine the psychological impact: surrounded by chaos, darkness, and fire, and there in the distance is a serene garden, lit and peaceful. It’s brought close enough to see clearly, but not yet to enter. This is part of the test, part of the waiting, part of what makes that Day unbearable for some and hopeful for others.

Ayah 14

عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ مَّآ أَحْضَرَتْ

After all these scenes—the cosmic collapse, the exposure of injustice, the opening of scrolls, the tearing of the sky, the blazing of Hell, the elevation of Paradise—everything culminates in this one profound statement:

Every soul will know what it has brought forward.

The word عَلِمَتْ means to know with certainty, to fully comprehend. The word نَفْسٌ refers to each individual soul, every single person. And أَحْضَرَتْ means what you brought to present, what you brought with you.

This seems contradictory at first. Other ayahs tell us the book will be handed to us—we don’t bring it ourselves. “They will find whatever they did standing in front of them.” So what does it mean that we “brought” it?

The answer is that you don’t need to read the book to know what’s in it. A previous surah already told us: “On that Day, the human being will remember completely everything they used to do.” Your memory will be perfect and complete. You will know without having to read a single word.

But there’s something deeper here. In this life, we do things without fully understanding their consequences or their true value. You yelled at your mother and left the house—but you don’t know that your younger brother learned from you and started yelling at her too. You created a pattern of behavior you didn’t even witness. Yet you’ll be held accountable for what you left behind.

Or perhaps you prayed in a parking lot during salah time. Someone saw you and made a video, confused about what you were doing. That video led someone to research Islam, which led to their conversion, which led to them becoming a scholar, which led to thousands accepting Islam through them. But you? You just prayed and left. You had no idea. On that Day, Allah ﷻ might tell you: “That prayer? That one got you into Paradise.” And you’ll be shocked because you were just praying quickly, in a hurry, and you had no idea of the chain reaction it started.

Some deeds you know you did, but you never realized their true weight or value. Some deeds you did but have no idea what came after them. Some deeds you did but are completely unaware you even did them—like simply smiling at someone entering the masjid, and that small gesture of warmth being the first positive interaction they had with a Muslim, changing their entire perception.

This is why we need to do deeds that create ripple effects of goodness beyond ourselves. Our lives are limited, but we can invest in actions that perpetuate good long after we’re gone. This is sadaqah jariyah—continuous charity through teaching, helping, setting good examples, doing anything that inspires or enables others toward good.

How do we know our good deeds outweigh our bad? How do we know our prayers are even accepted? We need deeds that compound and multiply. We need investments that keep growing.

Someone once asked the Prophet ﷺ: “When is the Hour?” He could have said he doesn’t know—he’s been told in the Quran not to claim knowledge of when Judgment Day will occur. But instead, he responded with a question: “What have you prepared for it?”

Sometimes the right response to a question is another question. The person asking “when is Judgment Day” is focused on the wrong thing. The Prophet ﷺ redirected them to what actually matters: not when it will happen, but whether you’re ready for it.

May Allah ﷻ make us among those who ask the right questions, or at least surround us with people who can guide our questions in the right direction. May He make us among those who prepare for that Day by investing in deeds that will continue to benefit us long after we’re gone.

Ayah 15–29 · linked video: 02. At-Takwir (Ayah 15-29)

Surah At-Takwir: Ayahs 15-29

Ayah 15

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلْخُنَّسِ

Allah ﷻ begins a profound series of oaths with “فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ” - “Then no, I swear.” This opening is significant because the particle فَ immediately connects this passage to what preceded it. When you encounter فَ in the Quran, it signals a deliberate link to the previous discussion. Here, after establishing the reality of the Day of Judgment and the opening of scrolls containing our deeds, Allah ﷻ transitions to address the other book - the Book of Revelation itself.

The Quran fundamentally refers to two books: the book of your deeds and the book of revelation. The previous passage focused on the first - the scrolls laid out on Judgment Day. Now Allah ﷻ turns our attention to the second - this Quran you’re hearing. The فَ here can be understood as “therefore” - because that Day is coming, I am establishing for you the authenticity of this revelation that will determine what appears in your book of deeds.

Allah ﷻ swears بِٱلْخُنَّسِ - by the disappearing stars. The word خُنَّس comes from a root meaning something that repeatedly appears and disappears. Stars twinkle in the night sky - they brighten, then dim, then brighten again. The word also carries another meaning: it describes how a hunted animal, like a fox, barely pokes its head out from hiding, then quickly retreats when spotted by hunters, only to appear again elsewhere. This image of something precious trying to survive while being pursued becomes deeply significant when we understand the complete picture Allah ﷻ is painting.

Ayah 16

ٱلْجَوَارِ ٱلْكُنَّسِ

These disappearing stars are further described as الْجَوَارِ - those that sweep across, moving in smooth fashion - and الْكُنَّسِ - those that enter and hide. This refers to shooting stars that blaze across the sky, then suddenly seem to enter into something and vanish completely. You see them clearly one moment, then they’re gone.

What is Allah ﷻ establishing through these celestial images? Previous revelations - the Torah given to Musa (AS), the Injeel given to Isa (AS), the scriptures of Ibrahim (AS) - these were lights in the darkness of human history. But what happened to them? They flickered. Their light was hunted down, suppressed, distorted. The Torah was completely destroyed at one point in history and had to be reconstructed from memory centuries later. The followers of Isa (AS) who believed in pure monotheism were persecuted to the ends of the earth. Their books were burned. The truth they carried barely survived, appearing here and disappearing there, like stars trying to shine while darkness pursued them.

Ayah 17

وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا عَسْعَسَ

Allah ﷻ continues: “And by the night as it عَسْعَسَ.” The word عَسْعَسَ describes that transitional moment when night is either darkening into complete blackness or beginning to lighten toward dawn. It’s that ambiguous hour when you could look at the sky and not know whether morning is approaching or evening is settling in.

In the context of these oaths, this refers to the end of the night - when the darkness that has dominated is finally beginning to recede. For 600 years before the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, no messenger had come. The last revelation was that of Isa (AS), and his true message had been hunted down and nearly extinguished. The world was in darkness with only occasional flickers of truth - like stars in a black sky. This was the night, and it was finally approaching its end.

Ayah 18

وَٱلصُّبْحِ إِذَا تَنَفَّسَ

“And by the morning as it تَنَفَّسَ - takes a breath.” The word نَفَسَ literally means to breathe, to exhale with relief. When morning arrives, it’s as though the world itself can breathe again after being suffocated by darkness. This is the most fresh, most pleasant, most revitalizing part of the entire day. Plants glisten with dew, animals awaken, life begins anew. The sun at this hour is gentle and welcoming, not harsh.

This is the Quran. After centuries of darkness, after previous revelations being hunted and corrupted, after truth barely surviving in scattered fragments, the final revelation has arrived - and it breathes new life into everything. The morning doesn’t flicker. The morning doesn’t hide. The morning illuminates everything, whether darkness likes it or not. The Arabs used to say, “أَضَاءَ الصُّبْحُ فَأَطْفَأَ الْمِصْبَاحَ” - “The morning appeared and made the lamp useless.” The Quran is that morning. Previous scriptures were necessary lights in the darkness, but now that dawn has come, now that the complete and final revelation is here, we don’t need to search for flickering lights anymore.

This imagery powerfully captures the nature of this final message: it is not running from anyone, it is not hiding, it is not being hunted down. It shines on everyone, reaching every corner of the earth. The security and permanence of this revelation is guaranteed by Allah ﷻ Himself.

Ayah 19

إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ

After these majestic oaths by elements of the heavens, Allah ﷻ delivers the conclusion: “Indeed, it is the word of a noble messenger.” The structure here is emphatic - إِنَّ followed by the lam of emphasis (لَ) before قَوْلُ. This is absolutely, certainly, undeniably the word being delivered by a noble messenger.

The word قَوْل is significant. In Arabic, there is قَوْل and there is كَلَام. Kalam is the original speech that originates from someone. Qawl is the word as it is delivered. When I recite these ayahs right now, the qawl comes from my mouth, but the kalam still belongs to Allah ﷻ. The Quran is the kalam of Allah ﷻ, but when Jibreel (AS) recites it, it becomes his qawl. When the Prophet ﷺ recites it, it becomes his qawl. The kalam never changes - it always remains Allah’s original speech - but the qawl refers to its delivery.

Who is this رَسُول كَرِيم - noble messenger? This is Jibreel (AS). The name Jibreel itself comes from Hebrew - “Gabri-El” - meaning “the powerful one of God” (جَبْر meaning power, and إِيل meaning God). What does Allah ﷻ call him here? ذِى قُوَّةٍ in the next ayah - possessor of power. The Quran is translating the very meaning of Jibreel’s name from Hebrew into Arabic, demonstrating that the source of both revelations is the same.

The description رَسُول كَرِيم establishes nobility as the first requirement. When a king sends a high-profile, classified message, who delivers it? Not just anyone - it must be a dignitary, someone of high rank and unquestionable character. The message of the Quran is the highest-profile communication ever sent from the heavens to earth, and so its courier must possess كَرَامَة - nobility and honor.

Ayah 20

ذِى قُوَّةٍ عِندَ ذِى ٱلْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ

The noble messenger is further described as ذِى قُوَّةٍ - possessor of great might and power. Why is power important for a messenger? Because this is classified, crucial information that someone might try to intercept or corrupt. When high-security documents are transported in this world, armed guards surround them. But Jibreel (AS) needs no guards - he himself possesses overwhelming power. No one can overpower him and steal or alter the message he carries.

Then Allah ﷻ describes him as عِندَ ذِى ٱلْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ - stationed in the company of the Owner of the Throne, firmly established there. This addresses proximity and access. When delivering a highly sensitive message, you cannot have multiple links in the chain where the message could be compromised. The messenger must have direct access to the highest authority. Jibreel (AS) is right there by the Throne itself - there is no intermediary between him and Allah ﷻ. He receives the revelation directly from the highest source.

The word مَكِين is profound. It comes from تَمْكِين and مَكَانَة, indicating someone of extremely high status, someone permanently established and secure in their position. Like someone with unquestionable tenure, someone whose rank is recognized and respected by all, someone who has been there and whose authority cannot be challenged. This is Jibreel’s (AS) station - firmly established in the highest realm, trusted completely.

Ayah 21

مُّطَاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ

Jibreel (AS) is مُطَاع - obeyed there. When he descends with the revelation, he comes with a legion of angels. The previous surah mentioned the angels who write down the revelation - كِرَامٍ كَاتِبِينَ. Other places in the Quran describe how when revelation descended, the entire sky was filled with angels, and the jinn who used to eavesdrop near the heavens found themselves completely blocked. Imagine a presidential motorcade - streets are blocked for miles around, communications are scrambled, the entire area goes on lockdown. This is the level of security provided when the Quran descended from the Throne to earth. Jibreel (AS) led this angelic convoy, and they all obeyed his command.

Then comes the final, most crucial characteristic: أَمِينٍ - absolutely trustworthy. You could have nobility, power, proximity, a security detail, and high rank, but if the messenger himself is not honest, all of that means nothing. If an ambassador is corrupt, he could sell the information, alter the message, or add his own opinions. The word أَمِين describes someone who guards the trust (أَمَانَة) completely, who doesn’t change even a single word, who delivers the message exactly as received regardless of personal feelings or politics.

Notice the progression: noble, powerful, stationed by the Throne, obeyed, and trustworthy. Every possible measure has been described to demonstrate the absolute security and authenticity of this revelation’s transmission.

Ayah 22

وَمَا صَاحِبُكُم بِمَجْنُونٍ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the second messenger in this chain - Muhammad ﷺ - but notice how He addresses it: “And your companion is not at all possessed or insane.” The word صَاحِب - your companion, the one who has lived his entire life among you - is powerful. Allah ﷻ doesn’t need to establish Muhammad’s ﷺ honesty, trustworthiness, or nobility. Why? Because the people already know these qualities. They themselves called him الصَّادِق الأَمِين - the truthful, the trustworthy.

The allegation against him wasn’t about his character - they couldn’t attack that. Their claim was that the words he recited couldn’t possibly be his own, so they must be coming from a jinn possessing him. The word مَجْنُون literally means someone affected by jinn. Allah ﷻ categorically denies this: your companion who you’ve known your entire lives, who has never shown any sign of insanity, who you trusted with your most valuable possessions, is not possessed at all.

The argument is devastating: you know his character, you know his history, and what he’s bringing you is so profound and coherent that to attribute it to madness or demonic possession is absurd.

Ayah 23

وَلَقَدْ رَءَاهُ بِٱلْأُفُقِ ٱلْمُبِينِ

“And certainly he saw him” - Muhammad ﷺ saw Jibreel (AS) - “on the clear horizon.” The word أُفُق refers to the horizon where sky meets earth, and مُبِين means absolutely clear, unmistakable. This refers to the Prophet’s ﷺ overwhelming experience when Jibreel (AS) first appeared to him. After the initial revelation in the cave, the Prophet ﷺ emerged to find that Jibreel (AS) had taken over the entire visible sky. Everywhere he looked, there was only Jibreel (AS) - filling the horizon completely.

This experience would drive anyone to madness. The fact that the Prophet ﷺ maintained his composure, went home to his wife Khadijah (RA), found peace with her (as Allah ﷻ says, “that you may find peace in them”), and then continued to receive revelation with complete stability - this is proof of his soundness of mind and the authenticity of his experience.

Ayah 24

وَمَا هُوَ عَلَى ٱلْغَيْبِ بِضَنِينٍ

“And he is not ضَنِين concerning the unseen” - he doesn’t withhold or hoard information about the unseen. The word ضَنِين can mean both being stingy/cheap and being greedy/covetous. Both meanings apply here and provide different insights.

In Arab society, people claimed to have access to the unseen - fortune tellers, mystics, mediums. But what did they do? They’d give you a little information, then make you pay for more. They’d leave you in suspense, create cliffhangers, keep you coming back. “Your grandmother is speaking to me now… my hand is getting itchy” - meaning pay up and I’ll tell you more. They hoarded information to maintain their business.

The Prophet ﷺ doesn’t do this. When a surah is revealed, he recites the entire thing. When a story comes, he tells it completely. He’s not trying to sell you something piece by piece. He’s not creating suspense to keep you hooked. He delivers everything he receives, holding nothing back.

The other meaning - greedy or covetous - addresses those who seek out information about the unseen. Such people are all frauds. The authenticity of prophethood is that the Prophet ﷺ never sought this information. He wasn’t looking to discover the secrets of the cosmos or the nature of God or what happens after death. Allah ﷻ gave this to him. He didn’t pursue it. Anyone who claims to be seeking such information or offering to get it for you is a charlatan.

This is particularly relevant today when people pay money to so-called spiritual healers or those who claim to access the unseen. The moment someone charges a fee for “spiritual help” or claims they’ll find out hidden information for you, you have a problem. This is exactly what the Prophet ﷺ is not.

Ayah 25

وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطَـٰنٍ رَّجِيمٍ

“And it is not the word of a شَيْطَان رَجِيم” - a cursed, outcast devil. The word رَجِيم comes from رَجْم, which means to stone or to cast out. The devil has been expelled, rejected, cast away from the mercy of Allah ﷻ.

The word شَيْطَان itself comes from either شَطَّ (to be distant/extreme) or شَطَنَ (to burn with rage/jealousy). The devil operates through jealousy, rage, competition, and enmity. The Prophet ﷺ has none of these qualities. He’s not competing with anyone. The Quran isn’t here to impress anyone or prove superiority. It’s guidance, pure and simple.

By extension, when we represent the Quran, we shouldn’t be competing either. When someone tells me another speaker’s lectures are better than mine, I genuinely want to listen and benefit. Why would there be jealousy in sharing the Quran? Why have people turned religious knowledge into a competitive sport with rankings and competitions? We don’t even make distinctions between prophets - لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّن رُّسُلِهِ - so how can we justify jealousy among those serving Allah’s ﷻ book?

Everything that contains jealousy, hatred, or competition in religious work traces back to Shaytan, not to Allah ﷻ. We are all servants of this book, and there is no greater honor than that.

Ayah 26

فَأَيْنَ تَذْهَبُونَ

After establishing the complete authenticity of the revelation - its secure transmission through a noble, powerful, trustworthy angel to an honest, sane, authentic messenger - Allah ﷻ asks: “Then where are you going?” This question hits with full force after everything that preceded it.

You’ve been shown the reality of Judgment Day when your scrolls will be opened. You’ve been given the authentic word of Allah ﷻ through the most secure chain of transmission imaginable. You’ve seen that your Prophet ﷺ is truthful and not insane. So where exactly do you think you’re headed? Why are you walking away from this? What other path could possibly make sense?

The question is both bewildered and challenging. It connects back to the opening passage where everyone is running in confusion on Judgment Day with nowhere to go. Here, while there’s still time, Allah ﷻ is asking: where do you think you’re going to escape to?

Ayah 27

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

“It is nothing except a ذِكْر” - a reminder, a remembrance, a source of awareness - “for all nations, all peoples, all generations.” The word عَالَمِين encompasses everything - all times, all cultures, all societies, all ages, all ethnicities. This message is not limited to seventh-century Arabia. It’s not just for Arabs or just for that time period. It is universally applicable and relevant to every generation that will come until the Day of Judgment.

What does it remind them of? It reminds them of their purpose, their accountability, the truth of their existence. It’s perpetually relevant - whether you live in any society, any era, any part of the world, the Quran speaks to your reality and provides guidance for your circumstances. This is the miraculous nature of its universality.

Ayah 28

لِمَن شَآءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَسْتَقِيمَ

“For whoever wills among you to يَسْتَقِيم” - to go straight, to straighten up. But the word يَسْتَقِيم carries a deeper meaning than just “going straight.” It comes from قَامَ - to stand upright. It means to stand up from a lying position, to elevate yourself, to rise up.

This is already a reference to the elevation toward heaven. When you stand straight and walk upward, where are you headed? Toward meeting with Allah ﷻ. But notice something about heading upward - gravity works against you. Going uphill requires effort and strength. Allah ﷻ is saying whoever is strong-willed enough, whoever has made their determination solid, they can take this upward path.

The word used here is شَاءَ, which is stronger than أَرَادَ. شَاءَ comes from شَيْءٌ - something tangible and solid. It’s not just a weak intention or wishful thinking. It’s a firm, executed decision. Whoever has solidified their will and determination, the path is open for them.

Ayah 29

وَمَا تَشَآءُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

“And you do not will except that Allah ﷻ wills, the Lord of all nations and peoples.” This ayah has caused confusion for many people. On the surface, it seems to say: you can’t will anything except what Allah ﷻ wills, which sounds like we have no free will at all. But that’s a misunderstanding.

This statement appears three times in the Quran, always in the context of the afterlife. Notice that this surah began with Judgment Day. When that Day comes, no matter what you want, you won’t be able to execute it. You might want to run this way or that way, but you’ll be herded and paired up as Allah ﷻ described earlier. Eventually, what will you end up doing? Only what Allah ﷻ allows and decrees for that Day.

But there’s another way to understand this even in this worldly life. Imagine I tell my children: “You can do whatever you want for one hour inside the house.” Now they’re exercising their free will, doing what they choose. But is their free will still under my authority? Yes - the only reason they’re allowed to exercise their will is because I permitted it. I willed that they should have free will within certain boundaries.

Similarly, Allah ﷻ has decreed that we have a period of time to make choices. What are we doing? Exercising the free will that Allah ﷻ has granted us. Eventually, He will take that choice away. We’re operating within the room Allah ﷻ has given us to make our decisions. And ultimately, whether we like it or not, we will end up where He wills - either in His mercy or His justice, based on how we used the free will He granted us.

This isn’t about fatalism or having no responsibility. It’s about recognizing that our very ability to choose is itself a gift from Allah ﷻ, and that gift comes with boundaries and with an eventual end when we’ll face the consequences of how we used it.

The surah’s structure reveals remarkable coherence. It opens with Judgment Day and scrolls being opened - the book of our deeds. It concludes with the question of where we’re going and reminds us we’ll only go where Allah ﷻ wills - back to those opened scrolls and their consequences. In between these bookends, the middle passage establishes the authenticity of the revelation - the other book, the Book of Allah ﷻ.

The first passage describes stars falling and the sky being torn apart. The middle passage describes stars twinkling and morning breaking - reminding us that while we still have time, we should respond to this light. The contrast is deliberate: Judgment hasn’t happened yet, the sky is still intact, there’s still time to act.

Both passages about Judgment Day (opening and closing) deal with the book of our deeds and where we’ll end up. The central passage deals with the book of revelation that will determine what’s written in our book of deeds. This revelation - transmitted with perfect security through a noble, powerful, trustworthy angel to an honest, sane, authentic prophet - is the morning that has brought relief after centuries of darkness. It is our only path to salvation on that Day when the scrolls open and we have nowhere to run.

May Allah ﷻ secure our faith in this revelation. May He make us and the next generation true ambassadors of this faith to others.
deep:
Ayah 1–14 · linked video: 01. At-Takwir (Ayah 1-14)

Root Word Analysis

The word kuwwirat (كُوِّرَتْ) comes from the root k-w-r (ك-و-ر), originally meaning to fold, wrap, or roll up something. Arabs used this word when folding up their turbans - taking a rectangular cloth, folding it, then wrapping it. This same word applied to the sun suggests either the visible folding/disappearing of the sun itself, or the diminishing of sunlight reaching Earth.

The word inkadarat (انكَدَرَتْ) from root k-d-r has dual meanings: first, it refers to something becoming dirty or polluted (like water changing color when it goes bad - kadara). Second, it means something being plucked or pulled out suddenly, like a horse’s hoof striking sand on a beach and sending grains flying (al-khatra). This creates a dual image of stars both changing color (appearing dirty) and being violently extracted from the sky.

Suyyirat (سُيِّرَتْ) comes from the root meaning to make something sail or move smoothly. The passive form indicates mountains will be made to transition away, not violently destroyed but moved aside.

’Ushhar (عُشَّر) is specifically a she-camel pregnant for ten months, about to give birth. This was the most valuable possession in Arab society - more precious than any other animal because it represented doubled wealth. The root carries connotations of extreme value and careful attention.

Kuddirat (كُدِّرَتْ) refers to establishing value or price (sir). Sair in Arabic relates to pricing and evaluation. When applied to hellfire, it suggests hell being tested to its limits, its combustibility being evaluated and then ignited to maximum capacity - like determining how well fuel burns before setting it ablaze fully.

Azlafat (أُزْلِفَتْ) means brought close or elevated. The passive construction indicates Jannah will be brought near enough to be visible, creating a striking contrast with the chaos surrounding it.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

The entire opening section employs a critical grammatical inversion. The expected Arabic construction would be tukkawwaru ash-shamsu (verb-noun order), but the Quran uses ash-shamsu kuwwirat (noun-verb order). This taqdim wa ta’khir (fronting and delaying) serves multiple rhetorical purposes: it expresses shock, amazement, and emphasis. The translation attempting to capture this would be: “Even the sun - it will be folded up!” with double exclamation marks implied.

Every single ayah from 1-13 begins with this unusual ism-fi’l (noun-verb) construction instead of the grammatically expected fi’l-ism pattern. This repetitive stylistic choice amplifies the emotional intensity throughout the passage.

The use of idha (إِذَا) is significant - it indicates future events with certainty, unlike in (إِنْ) which refers to past conditionals. Though the verbs appear in past tense (kuwwirat, inkadarat, suyyirat), the presence of idha transforms them to future meaning. This past-tense-for-future construction serves two purposes: it guarantees the occurrence (“consider it done” mentality in English), and it suggests sudden, immediate action when these events transpire.

The structure employs sharṭ wa jawab ash-sharṭ (condition and response to condition) - a series of “when” statements (13 conditions) concluding with one “then” statement in ayah 14: ’alimat nafsun ma ahdarat (every soul will know what it brought forward). This creates sustained rhetorical tension that finally resolves.

The phrase bi ayyi dhanbin qutilat uses ayy (which/what) as an interrogative of impossibility - the baby girl committed no crime, making the question rhetorical and accusatory. The word dhanb (ذَنْب) literally means “tail” - suggesting sins that bring humiliation and degradation, not just any transgression but specifically shameful acts.

The progression from cosmic events (sun, stars, mountains, oceans) to human accountability (wa idha an-nufusu zuwwijat) follows a rhetorical pattern moving from macro to micro, from universal cataclysm to individual judgment.

Historical & Biblical Context

The practice of burying infant daughters alive (wa’d) was specific to certain pre-Islamic Arab tribes, though not universal among all Arabs. Two primary motivations drove this practice: First, the birth of a daughter was considered a challenge to manhood - proof the father wasn’t “man enough” to produce a son. Second, daughters were viewed as economic and political liabilities. Since girls would eventually marry into other tribes, fathers feared this would give those tribes leverage in future business dealings or conflicts. If a father had disputes with the tribe his daughter married into, they could mistreat his daughter, and his wife would pressure him to concede to protect their child.

The less severe practice involved complete social isolation - the mother and daughter would be placed in a separate tent for life, with food left outside. The father would never see either of them again, considering the mother cursed for producing a female child.

The more heinous practice involved taking the girl to the outskirts of the settlement, digging a ditch, and burying her alive while she screamed - this was considered proof of the father’s “manhood” that he could withstand her cries. The girls were old enough to comprehend what was happening, making their terror and screams part of the horrific ritual.

Historical poetry preserves this reality. One abandoned woman sang to her daughter: “What’s wrong with Abu Hamza (the father)? He doesn’t visit, so angry we didn’t give him a boy. By Allah, that wasn’t in our hands. Women are like earth - we only give out what you put inside. If you’re angry about a girl, you’re the one who planted the seed.”

The Quran addresses this in Surah An-Nahl (16:58-59): “And when one of them is given news of a female, his face becomes dark and he is filled with inward grief. He hides from the people because of the evil of what he was given news about. Should he keep her in humiliation, or bury her in the dust?”

Significantly, Allah chooses to highlight this crime - committed against children in a pre-Islamic, disbelieving society - as the first human injustice mentioned on Judgment Day. This demonstrates the Quran’s universal concern for human rights and oppression regardless of the victims’ religious status. The Quran doesn’t say “that’s the kuffar’s problem” - it centers the most vulnerable victim in its depiction of divine justice.

No specific Biblical parallels to this practice exist, though child sacrifice to Molech in ancient Canaanite religion shares thematic similarities of destroying children for perceived benefits.

Coherence

Surah At-Takwir serves as the first in a sequence of eschatological surahs (At-Takwir, Al-Infitar, Al-Inshiqaq) that progressively reveal Judgment Day scenes. Each surah contributes pieces of the puzzle, with At-Takwir establishing the foundational imagery.

The surah divides into three distinct sections: (1) Ayahs 1-14: cosmic and earthly upheaval concluding with individual accountability; (2) Ayahs 15-26: the nature of revelation and the Prophet’s ﷺ mission; (3) Ayahs 27-29: concluding affirmations about divine will and human responsibility.

The internal progression moves systematically: sky events (sun folding, stars falling) → earth events (mountains moving, oceans merging) → animal behavior (pregnant camels abandoned, wild beasts herded together) → human sorting (souls paired up) → resurrection (buried girl questioned) → judgment infrastructure (scrolls opened, sky peeled, hell ignited, Jannah brought near) → individual realization (every soul knowing what it brought forward).

This creates a cinematic sequence from cosmic to intimate, universal to personal. The mention of al-maw’udah (the buried girl) serves as the moral center, positioned exactly between the physical cataclysms and the judgment proceedings.

The connection to the previous surah (’Abasa) is direct: Surah ’Abasa concluded with “yawma yafirru al-mar’u min akhihi… likulli imri’in minhum yawma’idhin sha’nun yughnihi” (the Day a person will flee from his brother… every person that Day will have a matter adequate to preoccupy him). At-Takwir opens by depicting exactly what preoccupies people - cosmic chaos so overwhelming that the most valuable possession (pregnant camel) is abandoned without thought.

Nazm (thematic coherence) with other Judgment Day descriptions: The same mountains mentioned here (idha al-jibalu suyyirat) appear in Surah At-Tur as wa sayyarat al-jibalu sayra and in Surah Al-Waqi’ah as wa bussat al-jibalu bassa. The Quran uses different verbs (suyyirat/sailed, bussat/crumbled) to describe complementary aspects - first they’re moved, then they’re pulverized.

The nufus zuwwijat (souls paired) connects to Surah Yasin’s uhshuru alladhina zalamu wa azwajahum (gather those who wronged and their kinds) and Surah Al-Waqi’ah’s three categories (ashabu al-maymanah, ashabu al-mash’amah, as-sabiqun). The pairing could mean: body reunited with soul, individuals paired with their personal shayatin (as in Surah Qaf), or people grouped by similar characteristics/deeds.

Psychological, Political & Social

The psychological portrait painted here reveals how extreme crisis completely reorders human priorities. The pregnant camel (’ishar) represents the pinnacle of Arab wealth - yet it’s abandoned without thought. This demonstrates that attachment to worldly possessions is contextual, not absolute. When faced with existential terror, what we deemed invaluable becomes instantly worthless.

The behavioral description of the father upon hearing news of a daughter’s birth (wajhuhu muswaddan wa huwa kadhim) - face darkened, swallowing rage, avoiding people - exposes toxic masculinity’s psychological mechanisms: shame internalization, anger suppression leading to violence, and social withdrawal to avoid perceived humiliation. This pattern still manifests today when patriarchal societies view daughters as burdens.

The practice of wa’d reveals social dynamics where: (1) male honor is constructed around control and “productivity” of male heirs, (2) women are commodified as potential leverage points between tribes, (3) economic anxiety drives elimination of perceived liabilities, (4) mothers internalize misogyny and perpetuate daughter-devaluation (as noted - often worst oppression against women comes from other women).

The question “bi ayyi dhanbin qutilat” (for what crime was she killed) psychologically reverses perpetrator and victim - forcing the murderer to confront the innocence of the murdered. This is restorative justice on a cosmic scale, giving voice to the voiceless.

The contemporary application addresses: educational inequality (denying girls education = burying them alive intellectually), forced marriages (burying their agency and humanity), son preference (creating psychological trauma in daughters who internalize being “less valuable”), and family honor systems that sacrifice daughters’ wellbeing for social reputation.

The mention of qarin (personal shaytan) introduces individual psychological warfare - each person has a devil who studies their unique vulnerabilities and strikes at opportune moments. This acknowledges that temptation is personalized and persistent, not random.

The concept of ma ahdarat (what you brought forward) psychologically shifts focus from anxiety about judgment to accountability for preparation. When asked “when is the Hour?” the Prophet ﷺ responds “ma a’dadta laha” (what have you prepared for it?) - redirecting obsession with timing toward obsession with readiness.

The ripple effect of deeds (someone praying in a parking lot leading to thousands accepting Islam through an unknown chain of events) demonstrates that moral value isn’t determined by immediate perception but by consequences we cannot see. This should inspire both hope (our small good deeds may compound exponentially) and caution (our small sins may likewise multiply).

Socially, the surah establishes universal human rights - Allah defends the murdered infant girl before even mentioning believers’ accountability. This contradicts tribalistic mentality of “their problems don’t concern us.” The Quran demonstrates concern for child abuse, gender-based violence, and social injustice as divine priorities regardless of the victims’ religion.

Quranic Wisdom

The fundamental wisdom embedded in these ayat is the certainty and suddenness of divine reckoning. The use of past-tense verbs for future events (kuwwirat, inkadarat, suyyirat) teaches that Allah’s promise is as certain as completed actions - “consider it done.” This cultivates taqwa by making the unseen as real as the seen.

The imagery of cosmic upheaval - sun folding, stars falling, mountains sailing - serves to shatter false security in the permanent. What appears most stable (mountains) and most constant (celestial bodies) will be violently transformed. This wisdom teaches detachment from dunya not through asceticism, but through realistic recognition of its temporary nature.

The abandoned pregnant camel (wa idha al-’isharu ’uttilat) offers a profound metaphor: even your most valuable investment will mean nothing when true crisis arrives. We pursue wealth, status, comfort as if they’re ultimate - but one day, they’ll be like abandoned camels. This should calibrate our life priorities now.

The pairing of souls (zuwwijat) with multiple meanings (body reunited with soul, paired with personal shaytan, grouped with similar people, categorized by deeds) teaches that nothing about us is isolated. Our souls don’t exist independently - they’re interwoven with our physicality, our spiritual opponents, our social circles, and our accumulated choices. Resurrection is holistic.

The centering of al-maw’udah’s question reveals Allah’s concern for the most vulnerable and voiceless. Before scrolls open, before hell blazes, before people’s deeds are reckoned - a murdered infant girl gets to ask her killers “why?” This establishes that divine justice prioritizes victims, especially those who had no power to defend themselves in this life.

The question itself - bi ayyi dhanbin qutilat (for what crime?) - is unanswerable, which is precisely the point. It forces confrontation with the absolute innocence of the victim and the absolute injustice of the crime. Some questions aren’t meant to be answered but to expose truth through their impossibility.

The description of hell being su’’irat (tested/ignited to capacity) while Jannah is uzlifat (brought near/elevated) teaches that both destinations already exist and will be activated/revealed on that Day. This means our choices now are selecting between two real, existing eternal outcomes - not theoretical possibilities.

The final statement - ’alimat nafsun ma ahdarat (every soul will know what it brought forward) - contains liberation and warning. You won’t need anyone to tell you your worth; you’ll know. The deeds whose consequences you never saw (ma jahilat, things you were unaware of) will be revealed. That Costco parking lot prayer that led to thousands accepting Islam, that smile to a stranger that changed their perception - these hidden sadaqat jariyah will emerge.

This teaches investment strategy for etakhira: pursue deeds with ripple effects - teaching someone, helping someone, leaving good examples. These create chains of goodness beyond our lifetime, exponentially multiplying our scale on Judgment Day.

The Prophet’s ﷺ response to “when is the Hour?” with “ma a’dadta laha” (what have you prepared?) is timeless wisdom: don’t obsess over what you cannot control (timing); obsess over what you can control (preparation). Some questions deserve to be answered with better questions that redirect thinking.

The wisdom for our age: we bury daughters alive through educational denial, forced marriages, and cultivating shame around femininity. We create maw’udat in different forms. Allah will ask us the same unanswerable question: bi ayyi dhanbin? For what crime did you bury their potential, their agency, their humanity?

Ultimately, this surah teaches that Judgment Day isn’t arbitrary divine wrath - it’s the Day when reality becomes undeniable, when hidden consequences surface, when the oppressed speak and the oppressors answer, when the scales tip not by perception but by actual weight. Every action generated something - that Day, we’ll see exactly what we generated. May Allah make what we bring forward on that Day a source of joy, not regret.

Ayah 15–29 · linked video: 02. At-Takwir (Ayah 15-29)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 15

أُقْسِمُ (uqsimu) [ق-س-م]

The root ق-س-م means to swear or take an oath. The form أُقْسِمُ (first person singular) indicates Allah Himself is taking the oath, which elevates the significance of what follows. When Allah swears by His creation, it draws attention to the profound message that will be delivered.

الْخُنَّسِ (al-khunnas) [خ-ن-س]

The root خ-ن-س means to disappear, hide, or withdraw. Al-khunnas refers to something that repeatedly appears and disappears. This describes stars that twinkle or seem to vanish and reappear. The word also carries the meaning of an animal being hunted that pokes its head out and hides again, like a fox escaping hunters - barely appearing before disappearing once more.

Ayah 16

الْجَوَارِ (al-jawari) [ج-و-ر]

The root ج-و-ر relates to moving or flowing smoothly. Al-jawari describes celestial bodies that move in smooth fashion across the sky, particularly shooting stars that sweep across the horizon.

الْكُنَّسِ (al-kunnas) [ك-ن-س]

The root ك-ن-س means to sweep or enter into something. This describes how shooting stars appear to enter into something and disappear - they sweep across the sky and then seem to vanish as if entering another realm.

Ayah 17

اللَّيْلِ (al-layli) [ل-ي-ل]

The root ل-ي-ل refers to night. Here it’s mentioned with the definite article, indicating a specific quality or state of the night being referenced.

عَسْعَسَ (as’asa) [ع-س-ع-س]

This quadriliteral root means to approach or to change state. It can mean either when the night gets darker (approaching full darkness) or when the night starts to lose its darkness (approaching dawn). The context from the next ayah indicates it refers to the night transitioning toward morning - when the sky changes from black to blue.

Ayah 18

الصُّبْحِ (as-subhi) [ص-б-ح]

The root ص-ب-ح refers to morning or dawn. It represents the emergence of light after darkness, the beginning of a new day.

تَنَفَّسَ (tanaffasa) [ن-ف-س]

The root ن-ف-س means to breathe or take a breath. The form تَنَفَّسَ (Form V) intensifies this meaning - to take a deep breath of relief, to breathe freely. This personifies the morning as coming back to life, as if the night was choking on darkness and now the morning can breathe again. It suggests revival, renewal, and the restoration of life.

Ayah 19

قَوْلُ (qawlu) [ق-و-ل]

The root ق-و-ل means to say or speak. Qawl refers to a specific utterance or word - the recited speech. This is distinct from كَلَام (kalam) which means speech in general. The Quran is the كَلَام of Allah, but when Jibreel (AS) speaks it or the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم recites it, it becomes their قَوْل while remaining Allah’s كَلَام.

رَسُولٍ (rasulin) [ر-س-ل]

The root ر-س-ل means to send or dispatch. A rasul is a messenger - one who is sent with a message. Here it refers to Jibreel (AS), the angelic messenger who delivers revelation from Allah to the prophets.

كَرِيمٍ (karimin) [ك-ر-م]

The root ك-ر-م means to be noble, generous, or honored. Karim describes someone of high status, dignity, and honor. This establishes Jibreel’s (AS) nobility and trustworthiness as a messenger.

Ayah 20

ذِي (dhi) [و-ذ-ي]

This means possessor of or owner of. It connects a person to a quality they possess.

قُوَّةٍ (quwwatin) [ق-و-ي]

The root ق-و-ي means strength, might, or power. Quwwa emphasizes great physical or spiritual strength. This describes Jibreel’s (AS) immense power and capability. Interestingly, the Hebrew name Gabriel (Gavri’el) means “power of God” - and Allah translates this very name by describing Jibreel (AS) as possessing قُوَّة.

عِنْدَ (inda) [ع-ن-د]

Means “with” or “in the company of” - indicating proximity and closeness.

ذِي الْعَرْشِ (dhi al-arsh) [ع-ر-ش]

Al-arsh is the Throne of Allah. Dhi al-arsh means the Owner or Possessor of the Throne - referring to Allah جل جلاله. This indicates Jibreel’s (AS) proximity to Allah’s throne, showing there is no intermediary between Allah and Jibreel (AS) in receiving revelation.

مَكِينٍ (makinin) [م-ك-ن]

The root م-ك-ن means to be firmly established, stable, or secure. Makin describes someone with a secure, high-ranking position - someone who has tenure and whose authority is respected. Jibreel (AS) has an established, unquestionable rank in the heavens.

Ayah 21

مُطَاعٍ (muta’in) [ط-و-ع]

The root ط-و-ع means to obey willingly. Muta’ (passive participle) means one who is obeyed - someone whose commands are followed. Jibreel (AS) leads a legion of angels who follow his instructions when delivering revelation.

ثَمَّ (thumma) [ث-م-م]

Means “there” or “in that place” - emphasizing location. It indicates that right there in the heavens, Jibreel (AS) is obeyed by other angels.

أَمِينٍ (aminin) [أ-م-ن]

The root أ-م-ن means to be safe, secure, or trustworthy. Amin is the ultimate characteristic of trustworthiness. This is the final and crucial quality - after all the power, proximity, and authority, Jibreel (AS) is completely trustworthy with the message. He will not alter, withhold, or corrupt any part of it.

Ayah 22

صَاحِبُكُمْ (sahibukum) [ص-ح-ب]

The root ص-ح-ب means to accompany or be a companion. Sahib is a companion - someone who has spent time with you. Allah uses this word to remind the Quraysh that Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم has lived his entire life among them. They know his character firsthand.

بِمَجْنُونٍ (bi-majnunin) [ج-ن-ن]

The root ج-ن-ن means to be hidden or covered, and from it comes jinn (hidden beings) and majnun (possessed or insane). Majnun literally means someone possessed by a jinn. The negation here (ما…بمجنون) emphatically denies that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is insane or possessed.

Ayah 23

رَآهُ (ra’ahu) [ر-أ-ي]

The root ر-أ-ي means to see or perceive. This confirms that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم actually saw Jibreel (AS) with his own eyes in his original form.

بِالْأُفُقِ (bil-ufuqi) [أ-ف-ق]

The root أ-ف-ق refers to the horizon - the furthest point where earth meets sky. Al-ufuq indicates the expansive horizon.

الْمُبِينِ (al-mubini) [ب-ي-ن]

The root ب-ي-ن means to be clear, evident, or manifest. Al-mubin means the clear, open horizon - nothing obscured the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم vision. Jibreel (AS) filled the entire visible sky in clear view.

Ayah 24

ضَنِينٍ (daninin) [ض-ن-ن]

The root ض-ن-ن means to withhold miserly or to be stingy and greedy. Danin describes someone who hoards information or sells it reluctantly. The negation (وَمَا هُوَ…بِضَنِينٍ) means the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم does not withhold or charge for knowledge of the unseen. He freely shares all revelation he receives, neither holding back information to create suspense nor seeking payment for spiritual knowledge.

الْغَيْبِ (al-ghaybi) [غ-ي-ب]

The root غ-ي-ب means to be absent or hidden. Al-ghayb refers to the unseen realm - matters of the afterlife, divine knowledge, and spiritual realities hidden from human perception.

Ayah 25

بِقَوْلِ (bi-qawli) [ق-و-ل]

As explained in Ayah 19, qawl means a specific utterance or spoken word.

شَيْطَانٍ (shaytanin) [ش-ط-ن]

The root ش-ط-ن means to be distant or remote, and also relates to burning rage and extreme jealousy. Shaytan refers to Satan and devils - beings who are far from Allah’s mercy and burn with jealousy toward humanity.

رَجِيمٍ (rajimin) [ر-ج-م]

The root ر-ج-م means to stone or pelt with stones. Rajim means one who is cursed, outcast, or driven away - like someone being pelted with stones to chase them off. This describes the cursed, rejected state of devils.

Ayah 26

فَأَيْنَ (fa-ayna) [أ-ي-ن]

Ayna means “where” - asking about direction or destination. The ف (fa) connects this question to everything that preceded it: “Therefore, where…”

تَذْهَبُونَ (tadhhabuna) [ذ-ه-ب]

The root ذ-ه-ب means to go or depart. The question “where are you going?” implies they are walking away from the truth - heading in the wrong direction despite clear evidence.

Ayah 27

ذِكْرٌ (dhikrun) [ذ-ك-ر]

The root ذ-ك-ر means to remember or mention. Dhikr is a reminder or remembrance - something that brings to mind important truths. The Quran serves as a constant reminder to humanity.

لِلْعَالَمِينَ (lil-alamin) [ع-ل-م]

The root ع-ل-م relates to knowledge and knowing. Al-alamin (plural of alam) means all worlds, all peoples, all nations, all generations. This emphasizes the Quran’s universal applicability across time, place, culture, and ethnicity.

Ayah 28

لِمَنْ (li-man) [م-ن]

Means “for whoever” - indicating the Quran is available to anyone who chooses.

شَاءَ (sha’a) [ش-ي-أ]

The root ش-ي-أ means to will or wish strongly. Sha’a indicates a firm decision and strong will - not just a casual thought but determined intention.

يَسْتَقِيمَ (yastaqima) [ق-و-م]

The root ق-و-م means to stand upright or rise. The form يَسْتَقِيمَ (Form X) means to seek straightness or to stand upright. Interestingly, it implies standing upward - moving vertically toward the heavens, toward Allah. This requires strength of will because gravity (representing worldly pulls) works against upward movement.

Ayah 29

تَشَاءُونَ (tasha’una) [ش-ي-أ]

The same root as sha’a above - here in second person plural form meaning “you (all) will/wish.”

يَشَاءَ (yasha’a) [ش-ي-أ]

Third person singular - “He wills.” This is Allah’s will.

اللَّهُ (Allahu) [أ-ل-ه]

The supreme name of God - Al-Ilah (The God), contracted to Allah. The One worthy of all worship.

رَبُّ (rabbu) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means to nurture, sustain, and have authority over. Rabb means Lord, Master, Sustainer - the One who creates, provides for, and governs all creation.

الْعَالَمِينَ (al-alamin) [ع-ل-م]

Repeated from Ayah 27 - all worlds, all peoples, all nations. This final ayah emphasizes that ultimate authority belongs to Allah, the Master of everyone and everything.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayahs 15-18

The opening particle فَلَا (fa-la) combines ف (fa - “then/therefore”) with لَا (la - negation for emphasis), creating “No! I swear…” This construction emphatically transitions from the previous passage while intensifying the oath. The ف connects this section to what came before - because Judgment Day is real, therefore I swear by these celestial signs that this revelation is authentic.

The oath structure uses the verb أُقْسِمُ (uqsimu - I swear) followed by بِ (bi - by) governing three parallel objects: بِالْخُنَّسِ (by the disappearing ones), الْجَوَارِ الْكُنَّسِ (the smoothly moving, entering ones), وَاللَّيْلِ إِذَا عَسْعَسَ (and by the night when it changes), وَالصُّبْحِ إِذَا تَنَفَّسَ (and by the morning when it breathes). This creates a rhythmic progression through four celestial phenomena.

The use of إِذَا (idha - when) in Ayahs 17-18 introduces temporal clauses describing specific states: when the night transitions and when the morning takes its breath. This emphasizes particular moments of transformation rather than general states.

The verb تَنَفَّسَ (tanaffasa - took a breath) is Form V, which intensifies the basic meaning of breathing. This creates vivid personification - the morning doesn’t just arrive, it breathes with relief, as if coming back to life after suffocation. This rhetorical device (personification/tashbih) makes the abstract concept of dawn emotionally resonant.

The progression from disappearing stars to moving stars to night changing to morning breathing creates a cinematic sequence - we watch the sky transform from darkness with twinkling lights to full illumination. This visual progression mirrors the spiritual progression from previous scriptures (dim flickering lights) to the Quran (full morning light).

Ayah 19

The response to the oath begins with إِنَّهُ (innahu - indeed it), where إِنَّ is a particle of emphasis and confirmation. The هُ (hu - it) refers back to an unstated but understood subject: the Quran, the revelation, these words you are hearing.

The structure لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (la-qawlu rasulin karimin - surely the word of a noble messenger) uses the lam of emphasis (ل) before قَوْل, adding another layer of assertion. This isn’t casual speech - this is sworn testimony.

The indefinite form رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (a messenger, a noble one) with the descriptive adjective كَرِيمٍ creates emphasis through tanwin (nunation). The quality of nobility is intrinsic to the messenger’s identity.

The word order is significant: قَوْلُ (word) comes before رَسُولٍ (messenger), emphasizing that this is about the message’s authenticity first, then identifying its noble carrier.

Ayah 20

This ayah consists of a string of descriptive phrases all modifying رَسُولٍ from the previous ayah: ذِي قُوَّةٍ (possessor of strength), عِندَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ (with the Owner of the Throne), مَكِينٍ (firmly established). These are construct phrases (idafa) and prepositional phrases that build a profile of Jibreel’s (AS) credentials.

The phrase عِندَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ uses عِندَ (with/in the presence of) to indicate proximity to Allah’s throne. This is not just “near” but “in the direct company of” - emphasizing there are no intermediaries between Allah and Jibreel (AS).

The adjective مَكِينٍ (firmly established) is in the indefinite form with tanwin, but its position at the end of the ayah and its pause (waqf) gives it emphatic weight - he is established, period.

Ayah 21

The structure مُطَاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ (obeyed there, trustworthy) uses two more indefinite adjectives with tanwin. The particle ثَمَّ (there) is unusual - it’s not the typical وَثُمَّ (and then) for sequence, but ثَمَّ meaning “in that place” - emphasizing location. Right there in the heavens, he is obeyed.

The placement of أَمِينٍ (trustworthy) at the end creates a climactic finish to Jibreel’s (AS) description. After all the qualities of power, proximity, and authority, the final word is about moral character - trustworthiness. This is the capstone quality that makes all the others meaningful for delivering revelation.

Ayah 22

The structure shifts dramatically with وَمَا صَاحِبُكُم بِمَجْنُونٍ (and your companion is not insane). After describing Jibreel (AS) in third person, Allah now addresses the audience directly with the second person suffix كُم (your). This is iltifat (grammatical shifting) - changing from third person to second person to jolt the audience’s attention.

The word صَاحِبُكُم (your companion) is brilliant rhetoric. Instead of saying “the Prophet” or “Muhammad,” Allah says “YOUR companion” - the one who has lived among YOU. This forces them to acknowledge their personal knowledge of his character. He’s not a stranger; he’s YOUR sahib.

The particle بِ before مَجْنُونٍ creates the negation structure ما…ب which is emphatic - not at all insane, in no way possessed. The tanwin on مَجْنُونٍ makes it indefinite and general - he’s not insane in any sense of the word.

Ayah 23

وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ (wa-laqad ra’ahu - and certainly he saw him) uses the emphatic particle لَقَدْ (laqad - certainly, surely) with the past tense verb رَآهُ (he saw him). This is sworn testimony - he DEFINITELY saw him (Jibreel).

The structure بِالْأُفُقِ الْمُبِينِ (on the clear horizon) uses the definite article on both words - THE horizon, THE clear one - indicating a specific, known event. The adjective الْمُبِينِ (the clear) eliminates any possibility of doubt or confusion in the vision.

The progression from describing Jibreel’s (AS) heavenly status to now describing the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم earthly vision of him creates a connection between the heavenly and earthly realms. The revelation isn’t just claimed - it was witnessed.

Ayah 24

وَمَا هُوَ عَلَى الْغَيْبِ بِضَنِينٍ (wama huwa ala al-ghaybi bi-daninin - and he is not, regarding the unseen, withholding) uses the same emphatic negation structure as Ayah 22: ما…ب (not…at all).

The preposition عَلَى (ala - upon/regarding) governing الْغَيْبِ (the unseen) indicates his stance toward unseen knowledge - he stands over it not as a hoarder but as a distributor.

The choice of ضَنِينٍ (danin - stingy/greedy) over other words for withholding is deliberate. This word carries connotations of both miserliness (holding back to create artificial scarcity) and greed (charging for what should be free). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم does neither - he shares all revelation freely.

Ayah 25

وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَيْطَانٍ رَجِيمٍ (wama huwa bi-qawli shaytanin rajimin - and it is not the word of a cursed devil) uses the same emphatic negation structure for the third time in this passage.

Notice the parallelism: Ayah 19 stated positively what it IS (word of a noble messenger), and now Ayah 25 states negatively what it is NOT (word of a cursed devil). The contrast between كَرِيمٍ (noble) and رَجِيمٍ (cursed/outcast) is stark - the highest honor versus the lowest disgrace.

The shift from talking about the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم in Ayahs 22-24 back to talking about the revelation itself (هُوَ referring to the Quran) creates variety while maintaining thematic unity.

Ayah 26

فَأَيْنَ تَذْهَبُونَ (fa-ayna tadhhabuna - so where are you going?) uses the interrogative أَيْنَ (where) for direction. This is a rhetorical question expressing bewilderment - after all this evidence, where could you possibly be headed?

The particle ف (fa) connects this question to everything that preceded - THEREFORE, given all this evidence, where are you going? It’s both a consequence and a confrontation.

The shift to second person plural تَذْهَبُونَ (you all are going) directly addresses the audience, making them accountable. This is iltifat again - grammatical shifting for rhetorical impact.

The present tense تَذْهَبُونَ (you are going) suggests ongoing action - you are currently in motion, heading in a direction. The question challenges them to examine their trajectory.

Ayah 27

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَالَمِينَ (in huwa illa dhikrun lil-alamin - it is nothing but a reminder for all the worlds) uses the restrictive construction إِنْ…إِلَّا (not…except) meaning “nothing but” or “only.” This limits and defines the Quran’s nature precisely.

The indefinite ذِكْرٌ (a reminder) with tanwin emphasizes the quality itself - pure reminder, unadorned and simple in purpose.

The phrase لِّلْعَالَمِينَ (for the worlds) uses the lam of specification - FOR all peoples, all nations, all generations. The plural الْعَالَمِينَ (alameen) is comprehensive, including every possible group across time and space.

This ayah provides the thesis statement of the Quran’s purpose after dismantling objections - it’s not for one tribe or one time period; it’s a universal reminder.

Ayah 28

لِمَن شَاءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَسْتَقِيمَ (li-man sha’a minkum an yastaqima - for whoever among you wills to go straight) provides the condition for benefiting from this reminder.
